title: Ectopic Pregnancy — Ruptured
oneLiner: A fertilized embryo implants outside the uterine cavity (usually fallopian tube); rupture causes life-threatening intra-abdominal haemorrhage, the leading cause of first-trimester maternal death in the developed world.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with sudden-onset sharp lower abdominal pain, worse on the left side, and vaginal spotting. She is tachycardic (HR 115), hypotensive (BP 90/60), and pale. She reports her last period was 6 weeks ago. On examination, there is rebound tenderness and guarding in the left iliac fossa. She has shoulder tip pain on deep inspiration. A urine pregnancy test is positive.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues and meaning:**

- **Sudden sharp abdominal pain, localized one side** → suggests tubal distension and rupture; peritoneum irritated by blood
- **Vaginal spotting** → decidual shedding from endometrial instability; often scanty and dark (not heavy like miscarriage)
- **Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor** → hypovolaemic shock from intra-abdominal bleeding
- **Amenorrhea (6 weeks)** → gestational age where tubal rupture most common (6–8 weeks)
- **Positive pregnancy test** → must rule out ectopic until proven otherwise
- **Referred shoulder tip pain** → phrenic nerve irritation by blood under diaphragm (Kehr’s sign)
- **Rebound/guarding** → peritoneal irritation from haemoperitoneum
- **Cervical excitation (chandelier sign)** → also often present but not mentioned in classic vignette; indicates pelvic peritoneal irritation

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Syncope alone** – no abdominal pain, just collapse. Always test β-hCG in any woman of reproductive age.
- **Chronic ectopic (subacute)** – mild intermittent pain, small adnexal mass, evolving anaemia. Can mimic appendicitis or PID.
- **Nausea/vomiting, diffuse pain** – may be misdiagnosed as gastroenteritis. If pregnant, think ectopic until shock develops.
- **No vaginal bleeding** – 20% have no bleeding; absence of bleeding does not rule out.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
An ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized egg implants and grows outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (95%). When the growing embryo erodes through the tubal wall, it ruptures the tube and lacerates adjacent blood vessels, causing torrential haemorrhage into the peritoneal cavity.

**Why this matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** ~1–2% of all pregnancies. Rupture occurs in 15–20% of ectopics, often before diagnosis.
- **Stakes:** Leading cause of maternal death in the first trimester and a major source of morbidity (infertility, blood transfusion, ICU stay).
- **Common pitfall:** Early pregnancy pain + bleeding is often dismissed as miscarriage; ectopic can kill within hours if not considered. Even a small rupture can cause profound shock due to the rich blood supply of the tubal vasculature.
- **Medico-legal trap:** Failure to perform a pregnancy test in a woman of childbearing age with abdominal pain or syncope is indefensible.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of ruptured ectopic pregnancy:**

- Implantation occurs in the tubal mucosa (usually ampulla). The trophoblast invades the wall, but the tube lacks the thick decidua and muscularis of the uterus.
- The embryo grows, distending the tube → intermittent colicky pain (tubal colic) from ischaemia and stretching.
- Eventually, the trophoblast erodes into a branch of the uterine or ovarian artery → rapid accumulation of haemorrhage into the tube (haematosalpinx).
- The tube cannot accommodate expanding blood → it ruptures, often during a sudden strain (cough, bowel movement, coitus), releasing 500–1500 mL of blood into the peritoneal cavity.

**Mechanism-to-Sign mapping:**

| Pathophysiological step | Clinical manifestation |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| Trophoblast invasion → erosion of tubal vessels | Sudden sharp pain at rupture |
| Blood in peritoneal cavity → irritation of parietal peritoneum | Rebound tenderness, guarding, cervical excitation |
| Haemorrhage → reduced venous return → compensatory tachycardia | Tachycardia, hypotension |
| Haemoperitoneum → irritation of phrenic nerve (C3–5) | Shoulder tip pain (Kehr’s sign) |
| Decreased circulating volume → decreased cerebral perfusion | Syncope, dizziness, pallor |
| Decidual instability (not endometrial breakdown) | Scanty vaginal bleeding (often dark brown) |
| Expanding haematoma in broad ligament (if rupture is contained) | Adnexal mass palpable on bimanual exam (uncommon in acute rupture) |

**Key concept:** The haemodynamic picture is disproportionately severe relative to the external bleeding. The patient may have minimal vaginal bleeding yet be in hypovolaemic shock – this is a major red flag.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
**Most common cause:** Prior tubal damage (especially from *Chlamydia trachomatis* infection) – accounts for ~50% of cases.

**Causes by category with stem clues:**

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infectious** | Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID), especially chlamydial salpingitis | History of STI, IUD use, multiple partners; previous PID diagnosis |
| **Tubal structural** | Previous tubal surgery (sterilization reversal, salpingostomy) | History of tubal ligation or salpingectomy for prior ectopic |
| **Iatrogenic** | Assisted reproductive technology (IVF/ICSI) | Patient conceived after embryo transfer; higher risk in tubal factor infertility |
| **Contraceptive** | Current intrauterine device (IUD) in situ | IUD present on examination – reduces uterine implantation but does not prevent tubal |
| **Congenital** | Tubal anomalies (diverticula, accessory ostia) | Young nulliparous woman; no risk factors – think anatomy |
| **Smoking** | Tobacco use (damages tubal cilia) | Smoker – risk increases with pack-years |
| **Idiopathic** | No identifiable risk factor | Most common category overall (40–50%) – always consider even in low-risk women |

**Notable:** Previous ectopic pregnancy – recurrence risk is 10% after one, 25% after two.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic approach in suspected ruptured ectopic:**  
**Time is critical.** Do not delay treatment for imaging if patient is unstable – go straight to theatre.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|---------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Urine β-hCG** | Positive (if >25 IU/L) | First step – confirms pregnancy | Any positive test in a woman with shock = ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise |
| **Serum β-hCG (quantitative)** | Level measured | Discriminatory zone – guides ultrasound interpretation | **Single value >1500 IU/L** (transvaginal) with empty uterus → ectopic. Below 1500, serial levels needed. |
| **Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)** | Empty uterine cavity, adnexal mass (ring of fire), free fluid in pouch of Douglas | Gold standard for diagnosis | **Triad: empty uterus + adnexal mass + free fluid** → 90% specific. Haemoperitoneum (anechoic fluid with echoes) suggests rupture. |
| **Full blood count** | Haemoglobin (low if significant bleed), WBC (may be raised) | Assess degree of blood loss and infection | Hb drop >1 g/dL in 1 hour suggests active bleeding. Do not rely on Hb alone – acute haemorrhage may not dilute yet. |
| **Blood group and crossmatch** | Type and antibody screen | Prepare for transfusion | Always order 2 units – may need emergency release |
| **Lactate** | Elevated in hypoperfusion | Marker of tissue hypoxia and shock severity | Lactate >2 mmol/L correlates with need for aggressive resuscitation |
| **Blood gases** | pH, base deficit | Metabolic acidosis from hypovolaemic shock | Base deficit >4 supports significant haemorrhage |

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
- **Clinical:** positive β-hCG + haemodynamic instability + abdominal pain → presume ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.
- **Imaging:** TVUS showing empty uterus, adnexal mass with yolk sac or fetal pole outside uterus.
- **Serial hCG:** If stable and β-hCG <1500 IU/L, repeat in 48h. In ectopic, rise is suboptimal (<66% in 48h) or plateau.

**Single finding that *rules out*:**  
- **Visible intrauterine pregnancy on TVUS with fetal cardiac activity** – but beware heterotopic (1:4000 spontaneously, 1:100 with IVF).

**Single finding that *confirms*:**  
- **Adnexal mass containing a yolk sac or fetal pole** on TVUS – definitive.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – treat as haemorrhagic shock until proven otherwise:**

1. **Call senior – gynaecology consultant and anaesthetist.** Do not order unnecessary tests.
2. **Large-bore IV access (×2)** – 14G green cannulae.
3. **Start fluid resuscitation** – warm crystalloid (IV ringer’s lactate 500 mL bolus, repeat based on haemodynamics). **Do not over-resuscitate** – target SBP 80–90 mmHg (permissive hypotension) until surgical control, to avoid diluting clotting factors.
4. **Crossmatch 4 units RBC** – order O-negative if unstable.
5. **Prepare for emergency laparoscopy or laparotomy** – surgical haemostasis is definitive.
6. **Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics** (e.g., cefuroxime 750 mg IV + metronidazole 500 mg IV) – cover peritonitis from infected haemoperitoneum.
7. **Administer anti-D immunoglobulin** (250–500 IU IM) if Rh-negative and Kleihauer test positive (or empirically if unstable).
8. **NO methotrexate** – contraindicated in ruptured ectopic (unstable, significant haemoperitoneum, large mass). Medical management is only for unruptured, small (<35 mm), β-hCG <5000 IU/L, no fetal cardiac activity, reliable patient.

**Operative approach:**  
- **Laparoscopy** preferred if stable and experienced surgeon – allows salpingectomy (remove affected tube) or salpingostomy (incise tube, remove product, preserve tube – higher recurrence, should be offered only when future fertility desired and contralateral tube absent/diseased).
- **Laparotomy** if unstable, massive haemoperitoneum, or laparoscopic expertise unavailable.
- **Salpingectomy is standard** in ruptured ectopic – tube is unsalvageable, and salpingostomy has higher failure (5–20%) and recurrence (15% vs 10% after salpingectomy).

**Ward (24–72h):**

- **Monitor Hb daily** – if post-op Hb <7 g/dL or ongoing transfusion need, consider re-look (missed second ectopic or bleeding from cystotomy).
- **Serial β-hCG** – should decline >15% by day 1; if it rises, suspect persistent trophoblast (needs methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM).
- **Pain control** – paracetamol 1 g IV q6h PRN; avoid NSAIDs (may mask signs).
- **Prophylactic antibiotics** – not needed post-op unless peritonitis present.

**Long-term:**

- **Fertility counselling** – advise that if contralateral tube appears normal, future pregnancy success rate ~60%. Recurrence risk 10–15% – should be managed in early pregnancy unit next time.
- **Methotrexate for persistent trophoblast** – if β-hCG fails to fall, give single dose (50 mg/m² IM) and repeat hCG weekly.
- **Psychological support** – loss of pregnancy and possible loss of tube is traumatic; refer to bereavement service.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

- ✗ Using methotrexate in unstable patient or with cardiac activity.
- ✗ Delaying surgery for imaging in a shocked patient.
- ✗ Assuming normal TVUS (empty uterus) is reassuring – you have not yet found the ectopic.
- ✗ Over-resuscitating with crystalloid before blood – leads to dilutional coagulopathy.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Ruptured Ectopic | Ovarian Cyst Rupture | Acute Appendicitis | Spontaneous Miscarriage | Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|--------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Sudden, sharp, unilateral; often shoulder tip | Sudden, unilateral, may be severe; no shoulder tip | Periumbilical → RLQ, gradual | Crampy, midline, intermittent | Bilateral lower abdominal, dull, ache |
| **Bleeding** | Scanty dark vaginal (decidual) | None or minimal | None | Heavy, bright red, clots | Mucopurulent cervical discharge |
| **Pregnancy test** | Positive | Negative | Negative | Positive | Negative |
| **Signs of shock** | Present (tachy, hypotension, pallor) | Rare (if haemorrhagic cyst with significant bleed) | Absent (unless severe sepsis) | Rare (if heavy bleed, but usually haemodynamically stable) | Absent (temperature may be raised) |
| **Examination** | Rebound, guarding, cervical excitation, adnexal mass (ill-defined) | Unilateral tenderness, mass felt if cyst large | McBurney’s point tenderness, Rovsing’s sign, psoas sign | Os open, uterus enlarged soft | Adnexal tenderness bilaterally, cervical motion tenderness |
| **TVUS** | Empty uterus, adnexal mass (ring of fire), free fluid | Complex cyst, internal echoes (clot) | Normal | Intrauterine gestational sac (if seen) | Thickened tubes, free fluid (not haemorrhagic) |
| **β-hCG** | Positive, suboptimal rise | Negative | Negative | Positive, doubling normal | Negative |
| **Key differentiator** | Shock + positive test + empty uterus | Negative test + acute pain + cyst on imaging | Negative β-hCG + RLQ pain + fever | Heavy bleeding + cervical os open | Negative β-hCG + cervical discharge + fever |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Hypovolaemic shock** | Collapse, hypotension, tachycardia, altered consciousness | Low BP, high HR, cold peripheries, lactate >4 | Massive transfusion protocol, immediate laparotomy, ICU |
| **Maternal death** | Usually from underestimation of blood loss – young women can compensate until irreversible | Sudden deterioration, cardiac arrest | Crash call, emergency thoracotomy/laparotomy to clamp pedicle |
| **Chronic ectopic / tubal abortion** | Slow bleed, recurrent pain, anaemia, adnexal mass | Hb drop over days, dull pain, β-hCG low and plateauing | Laparoscopy to remove mass; may need methotrexate if diagnosis delayed |
| **Infertility** | Loss of tube(s) – recurrence risk 15% after one ectopic | May need IVF | Refer to fertility specialist; consider salpingostomy if only tube |
| **Persistent trophoblast** | post-salpingostomy – failure to clear all trophoblast | Rising β-hCG after surgery | Methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM (single dose) |
| **Intra-abdominal abscess** | Contaminated haemoperitoneum | Fever, persistent pain, riger, raised WBC | CT-guided drainage or laparoscopy |

**Late-presentation pattern:** A woman who delayed seeking care may present days after a small rupture. She has an ileus, mild distension, dropping Hb, and a palpable mass in the adnexa (haematoma). She is at high risk for rupture on examination or during straining – handle gently.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for risk factors: "TUBAL ECTOPIC"**  
- **T** – Tubal surgery/salpingitis  
- **U** – Unexplained (idiopathic)  
- **B** – bBD (history of infertility/pelvic adhesions)  
- **A** – Assisted reproduction (IVF)  
- **L** – Ligation (failed tubal ligation)  
- **E** – Endometriosis (mild risk)  
- **C** – Contraceptive (IUD)  
- **T** – Tobacco smoking  
- **O** – Ovulation induction  
- **P** – Prior ectopic  
- **I** – Infection (Chlamydia / GC)  
- **C** – Congenital tubal anomalies  

**Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **"Any woman with syncope + abdominal pain = β-hCG until proven otherwise."** Even if she says she is on birth control.
2. **Shoulder tip pain = pathognomonic.** It means blood is under diaphragm → ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.
3. **"The lighter the vaginal bleeding, the more you worry about ectopic."** Heavy bleeding suggests miscarriage (products passing), scanty dark bleeding suggests decidual reaction.
4. **Cervical excitation ("chandelier sign") is classic but nonspecific – seen in PID, ruptured cyst, and ectopic.** If present + positive β-hCG = ectopic until proven.
5. **Pseudosac in the uterus can fool you.** An ectopic can produce a small intrauterine fluid collection from decidual reaction; do not call it a gestational sac unless you see a yolk sac or double decidual sign.
6. **If β-hCG >1500 and TVUS shows empty uterus, assume ectopic.** Do not repeat hCG – go to the OR or start methotrexate (if stable).
7. **Never use methotrexate in a woman with an ectopic mass >3.5 cm, fetal cardiac activity, or free fluid in peritoneum >100 mL.** These are surgical.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**

- **"She had a laparoscopic tubal ligation, so she can't be pregnant."** Wrong – failure rate 1–2%; ectopic is the typical failure mode.
- **"The pain is in the right upper quadrant, not the pelvis."** Phrenic irritation from blood under diaphragm can present as RUQ or shoulder pain – think ectopic.
- **"She had a normal TVUS yesterday, so she is fine."** An early ectopic may be invisible on ultrasound until it ruptures. Clinical suspicion overrides imaging.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant) immediately:**

- Any haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg, HR >110, lactate >2 mmol/L)
- GCS <15 or altered mental status
- NEWS2 score ≥5
- Positive β-hCG + collapsed patient
- Suspected ectopic but unable to confirm by TVUS (low hCG) – needs senior US review or diagnostic laparoscopy
- Rh-negative woman with anti-D not yet given
- Patient on anticoagulant therapy (higher risk of massive bleed)

**When to call ICU / crash team:**

- **Cardiorespiratory arrest** – start CPR, call crash team, prepare for emergency laparotomy (peri-mortem caesarean section not applicable here, but if uterus is gravid and >24 weeks, also consider perimortem C-section for maternal survival). Usually ruptured ectopic is early first trimester, so immediate laparotomy to clamp bleeding vessels is the priority.
- **Massive haemorrhage defined as >4 units RBC in 1 hour** – activate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 ratio of RBC, FFP, platelets).
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – reflects severe ischaemia and lactic acidosis requiring ICU monitoring.
- **Need for mechanical ventilation** – for airway protection or ongoing shock.

**Specific thresholds to know:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| SBP | <90 mmHg | IV access, fluid bolus, call senior |
| HR | >110 bpm | Presume hypovolaemia; crossmatch |
| Lactate | >2 mmol/L | Urgent intervention; >4 = ICU |
| Hb | <70 g/L (or drop >30 g/L in 4h) | Transfuse |
| NEWS2 | ≥5 | Escalate to registar; ≥7 = crash team |
| β-hCG not declining 15% by day 1 post-op | Persistent trophoblast | Call senior for methotrexate plan |

**Remember: ruptured ectopic kills by haemorrhage. The sooner you call for help and the more aggressively you control bleeding, the better the outcome. Do not wait for a formal diagnosis – treat the shocked pregnant woman as a ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.**

## Ectopic Pregnancy — Ruptured
**One-liner:** A fertilized embryo implants outside the uterine cavity (usually fallopian tube); rupture causes life-threatening intra-abdominal haemorrhage, the leading cause of first-trimester maternal death in the developed world.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with sudden-onset sharp lower abdominal pain, worse on the left side, and vaginal spotting. She is tachycardic (HR 115), hypotensive (BP 90/60), and pale. She reports her last period was 6 weeks ago. On examination, there is rebound tenderness and guarding in the left iliac fossa. She has shoulder tip pain on deep inspiration. A urine pregnancy test is positive.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues and meaning:**

- **Sudden sharp abdominal pain, localized one side** → suggests tubal distension and rupture; peritoneum irritated by blood
- **Vaginal spotting** → decidual shedding from endometrial instability; often scanty and dark (not heavy like miscarriage)
- **Tachycardia, hypotension, pallor** → hypovolaemic shock from intra-abdominal bleeding
- **Amenorrhea (6 weeks)** → gestational age where tubal rupture most common (6–8 weeks)
- **Positive pregnancy test** → must rule out ectopic until proven otherwise
- **Referred shoulder tip pain** → phrenic nerve irritation by blood under diaphragm (Kehr’s sign)
- **Rebound/guarding** → peritoneal irritation from haemoperitoneum
- **Cervical excitation (chandelier sign)** → also often present but not mentioned in classic vignette; indicates pelvic peritoneal irritation

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Syncope alone** – no abdominal pain, just collapse. Always test β-hCG in any woman of reproductive age.
- **Chronic ectopic (subacute)** – mild intermittent pain, small adnexal mass, evolving anaemia. Can mimic appendicitis or PID.
- **Nausea/vomiting, diffuse pain** – may be misdiagnosed as gastroenteritis. If pregnant, think ectopic until shock develops.
- **No vaginal bleeding** – 20% have no bleeding; absence of bleeding does not rule out.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
An ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized egg implants and grows outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (95%). When the growing embryo erodes through the tubal wall, it ruptures the tube and lacerates adjacent blood vessels, causing torrential haemorrhage into the peritoneal cavity.

**Why this matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** ~1–2% of all pregnancies. Rupture occurs in 15–20% of ectopics, often before diagnosis.
- **Stakes:** Leading cause of maternal death in the first trimester and a major source of morbidity (infertility, blood transfusion, ICU stay).
- **Common pitfall:** Early pregnancy pain + bleeding is often dismissed as miscarriage; ectopic can kill within hours if not considered. Even a small rupture can cause profound shock due to the rich blood supply of the tubal vasculature.
- **Medico-legal trap:** Failure to perform a pregnancy test in a woman of childbearing age with abdominal pain or syncope is indefensible.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of ruptured ectopic pregnancy:**

- Implantation occurs in the tubal mucosa (usually ampulla). The trophoblast invades the wall, but the tube lacks the thick decidua and muscularis of the uterus.
- The embryo grows, distending the tube → intermittent colicky pain (tubal colic) from ischaemia and stretching.
- Eventually, the trophoblast erodes into a branch of the uterine or ovarian artery → rapid accumulation of haemorrhage into the tube (haematosalpinx).
- The tube cannot accommodate expanding blood → it ruptures, often during a sudden strain (cough, bowel movement, coitus), releasing 500–1500 mL of blood into the peritoneal cavity.

**Mechanism-to-Sign mapping:**

| Pathophysiological step | Clinical manifestation |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| Trophoblast invasion → erosion of tubal vessels | Sudden sharp pain at rupture |
| Blood in peritoneal cavity → irritation of parietal peritoneum | Rebound tenderness, guarding, cervical excitation |
| Haemorrhage → reduced venous return → compensatory tachycardia | Tachycardia, hypotension |
| Haemoperitoneum → irritation of phrenic nerve (C3–5) | Shoulder tip pain (Kehr’s sign) |
| Decreased circulating volume → decreased cerebral perfusion | Syncope, dizziness, pallor |
| Decidual instability (not endometrial breakdown) | Scanty vaginal bleeding (often dark brown) |
| Expanding haematoma in broad ligament (if rupture is contained) | Adnexal mass palpable on bimanual exam (uncommon in acute rupture) |

**Key concept:** The haemodynamic picture is disproportionately severe relative to the external bleeding. The patient may have minimal vaginal bleeding yet be in hypovolaemic shock – this is a major red flag.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

**Most common cause:** Prior tubal damage (especially from *Chlamydia trachomatis* infection) – accounts for ~50% of cases.

**Causes by category with stem clues:**

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infectious** | Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID), especially chlamydial salpingitis | History of STI, IUD use, multiple partners; previous PID diagnosis |
| **Tubal structural** | Previous tubal surgery (sterilization reversal, salpingostomy) | History of tubal ligation or salpingectomy for prior ectopic |
| **Iatrogenic** | Assisted reproductive technology (IVF/ICSI) | Patient conceived after embryo transfer; higher risk in tubal factor infertility |
| **Contraceptive** | Current intrauterine device (IUD) in situ | IUD present on examination – reduces uterine implantation but does not prevent tubal |
| **Congenital** | Tubal anomalies (diverticula, accessory ostia) | Young nulliparous woman; no risk factors – think anatomy |
| **Smoking** | Tobacco use (damages tubal cilia) | Smoker – risk increases with pack-years |
| **Idiopathic** | No identifiable risk factor | Most common category overall (40–50%) – always consider even in low-risk women |

**Notable:** Previous ectopic pregnancy – recurrence risk is 10% after one, 25% after two.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic approach in suspected ruptured ectopic:**  
**Time is critical.** Do not delay treatment for imaging if patient is unstable – go straight to theatre.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|---------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Urine β-hCG** | Positive (if >25 IU/L) | First step – confirms pregnancy | Any positive test in a woman with shock = ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise |
| **Serum β-hCG (quantitative)** | Level measured | Discriminatory zone – guides ultrasound interpretation | **Single value >1500 IU/L** (transvaginal) with empty uterus → ectopic. Below 1500, serial levels needed. |
| **Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)** | Empty uterine cavity, adnexal mass (ring of fire), free fluid in pouch of Douglas | Gold standard for diagnosis | **Triad: empty uterus + adnexal mass + free fluid** → 90% specific. Haemoperitoneum (anechoic fluid with echoes) suggests rupture. |
| **Full blood count** | Haemoglobin (low if significant bleed), WBC (may be raised) | Assess degree of blood loss and infection | Hb drop >1 g/dL in 1 hour suggests active bleeding. Do not rely on Hb alone – acute haemorrhage may not dilute yet. |
| **Blood group and crossmatch** | Type and antibody screen | Prepare for transfusion | Always order 2 units – may need emergency release |
| **Lactate** | Elevated in hypoperfusion | Marker of tissue hypoxia and shock severity | Lactate >2 mmol/L correlates with need for aggressive resuscitation |
| **Blood gases** | pH, base deficit | Metabolic acidosis from hypovolaemic shock | Base deficit >4 supports significant haemorrhage |

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
- **Clinical:** positive β-hCG + haemodynamic instability + abdominal pain → presume ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.
- **Imaging:** TVUS showing empty uterus, adnexal mass with yolk sac or fetal pole outside uterus.
- **Serial hCG:** If stable and β-hCG <1500 IU/L, repeat in 48h. In ectopic, rise is suboptimal (<66% in 48h) or plateau.

**Single finding that *rules out*:**  
- **Visible intrauterine pregnancy on TVUS with fetal cardiac activity** – but beware heterotopic (1:4000 spontaneously, 1:100 with IVF).

**Single finding that *confirms*:**  
- **Adnexal mass containing a yolk sac or fetal pole** on TVUS – definitive.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – treat as haemorrhagic shock until proven otherwise:**

1. **Call senior – gynaecology consultant and anaesthetist.** Do not order unnecessary tests.
2. **Large-bore IV access (×2)** – 14G green cannulae.
3. **Start fluid resuscitation** – warm crystalloid (IV ringer’s lactate 500 mL bolus, repeat based on haemodynamics). **Do not over-resuscitate** – target SBP 80–90 mmHg (permissive hypotension) until surgical control, to avoid diluting clotting factors.
4. **Crossmatch 4 units RBC** – order O-negative if unstable.
5. **Prepare for emergency laparoscopy or laparotomy** – surgical haemostasis is definitive.
6. **Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics** (e.g., cefuroxime 750 mg IV + metronidazole 500 mg IV) – cover peritonitis from infected haemoperitoneum.
7. **Administer anti-D immunoglobulin** (250–500 IU IM) if Rh-negative and Kleihauer test positive (or empirically if unstable).
8. **NO methotrexate** – contraindicated in ruptured ectopic (unstable, significant haemoperitoneum, large mass). Medical management is only for unruptured, small (<35 mm), β-hCG <5000 IU/L, no fetal cardiac activity, reliable patient.

**Operative approach:**  
- **Laparoscopy** preferred if stable and experienced surgeon – allows salpingectomy (remove affected tube) or salpingostomy (incise tube, remove product, preserve tube – higher recurrence, should be offered only when future fertility desired and contralateral tube absent/diseased).
- **Laparotomy** if unstable, massive haemoperitoneum, or laparoscopic expertise unavailable.
- **Salpingectomy is standard** in ruptured ectopic – tube is unsalvageable, and salpingostomy has higher failure (5–20%) and recurrence (15% vs 10% after salpingectomy).

**Ward (24–72h):**

- **Monitor Hb daily** – if post-op Hb <7 g/dL or ongoing transfusion need, consider re-look (missed second ectopic or bleeding from cystotomy).
- **Serial β-hCG** – should decline >15% by day 1; if it rises, suspect persistent trophoblast (needs methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM).
- **Pain control** – paracetamol 1 g IV q6h PRN; avoid NSAIDs (may mask signs).
- **Prophylactic antibiotics** – not needed post-op unless peritonitis present.

**Long-term:**

- **Fertility counselling** – advise that if contralateral tube appears normal, future pregnancy success rate ~60%. Recurrence risk 10–15% – should be managed in early pregnancy unit next time.
- **Methotrexate for persistent trophoblast** – if β-hCG fails to fall, give single dose (50 mg/m² IM) and repeat hCG weekly.
- **Psychological support** – loss of pregnancy and possible loss of tube is traumatic; refer to bereavement service.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

- ✗ Using methotrexate in unstable patient or with cardiac activity.
- ✗ Delaying surgery for imaging in a shocked patient.
- ✗ Assuming normal TVUS (empty uterus) is reassuring – you have not yet found the ectopic.
- ✗ Over-resuscitating with crystalloid before blood – leads to dilutional coagulopathy.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Ruptured Ectopic | Ovarian Cyst Rupture | Acute Appendicitis | Spontaneous Miscarriage | Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|--------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Sudden, sharp, unilateral; often shoulder tip | Sudden, unilateral, may be severe; no shoulder tip | Periumbilical → RLQ, gradual | Crampy, midline, intermittent | Bilateral lower abdominal, dull, ache |
| **Bleeding** | Scanty dark vaginal (decidual) | None or minimal | None | Heavy, bright red, clots | Mucopurulent cervical discharge |
| **Pregnancy test** | Positive | Negative | Negative | Positive | Negative |
| **Signs of shock** | Present (tachy, hypotension, pallor) | Rare (if haemorrhagic cyst with significant bleed) | Absent (unless severe sepsis) | Rare (if heavy bleed, but usually haemodynamically stable) | Absent (temperature may be raised) |
| **Examination** | Rebound, guarding, cervical excitation, adnexal mass (ill-defined) | Unilateral tenderness, mass felt if cyst large | McBurney’s point tenderness, Rovsing’s sign, psoas sign | Os open, uterus enlarged soft | Adnexal tenderness bilaterally, cervical motion tenderness |
| **TVUS** | Empty uterus, adnexal mass (ring of fire), free fluid | Complex cyst, internal echoes (clot) | Normal | Intrauterine gestational sac (if seen) | Thickened tubes, free fluid (not haemorrhagic) |
| **β-hCG** | Positive, suboptimal rise | Negative | Negative | Positive, doubling normal | Negative |
| **Key differentiator** | Shock + positive test + empty uterus | Negative test + acute pain + cyst on imaging | Negative β-hCG + RLQ pain + fever | Heavy bleeding + cervical os open | Negative β-hCG + cervical discharge + fever |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Hypovolaemic shock** | Collapse, hypotension, tachycardia, altered consciousness | Low BP, high HR, cold peripheries, lactate >4 | Massive transfusion protocol, immediate laparotomy, ICU |
| **Maternal death** | Usually from underestimation of blood loss – young women can compensate until irreversible | Sudden deterioration, cardiac arrest | Crash call, emergency thoracotomy/laparotomy to clamp pedicle |
| **Chronic ectopic / tubal abortion** | Slow bleed, recurrent pain, anaemia, adnexal mass | Hb drop over days, dull pain, β-hCG low and plateauing | Laparoscopy to remove mass; may need methotrexate if diagnosis delayed |
| **Infertility** | Loss of tube(s) – recurrence risk 15% after one ectopic | May need IVF | Refer to fertility specialist; consider salpingostomy if only tube |
| **Persistent trophoblast** | post-salpingostomy – failure to clear all trophoblast | Rising β-hCG after surgery | Methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM (single dose) |
| **Intra-abdominal abscess** | Contaminated haemoperitoneum | Fever, persistent pain, riger, raised WBC | CT-guided drainage or laparoscopy |

**Late-presentation pattern:** A woman who delayed seeking care may present days after a small rupture. She has an ileus, mild distension, dropping Hb, and a palpable mass in the adnexa (haematoma). She is at high risk for rupture on examination or during straining – handle gently.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic for risk factors: "TUBAL ECTOPIC"**  
- **T** – Tubal surgery/salpingitis  
- **U** – Unexplained (idiopathic)  
- **B** – bBD (history of infertility/pelvic adhesions)  
- **A** – Assisted reproduction (IVF)  
- **L** – Ligation (failed tubal ligation)  
- **E** – Endometriosis (mild risk)  
- **C** – Contraceptive (IUD)  
- **T** – Tobacco smoking  
- **O** – Ovulation induction  
- **P** – Prior ectopic  
- **I** – Infection (Chlamydia / GC)  
- **C** – Congenital tubal anomalies  

**Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **"Any woman with syncope + abdominal pain = β-hCG until proven otherwise."** Even if she says she is on birth control.
2. **Shoulder tip pain = pathognomonic.** It means blood is under diaphragm → ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.
3. **"The lighter the vaginal bleeding, the more you worry about ectopic."** Heavy bleeding suggests miscarriage (products passing), scanty dark bleeding suggests decidual reaction.
4. **Cervical excitation ("chandelier sign") is classic but nonspecific – seen in PID, ruptured cyst, and ectopic.** If present + positive β-hCG = ectopic until proven.
5. **Pseudosac in the uterus can fool you.** An ectopic can produce a small intrauterine fluid collection from decidual reaction; do not call it a gestational sac unless you see a yolk sac or double decidual sign.
6. **If β-hCG >1500 and TVUS shows empty uterus, assume ectopic.** Do not repeat hCG – go to the OR or start methotrexate (if stable).
7. **Never use methotrexate in a woman with an ectopic mass >3.5 cm, fetal cardiac activity, or free fluid in peritoneum >100 mL.** These are surgical.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**

- **"She had a laparoscopic tubal ligation, so she can't be pregnant."** Wrong – failure rate 1–2%; ectopic is the typical failure mode.
- **"The pain is in the right upper quadrant, not the pelvis."** Phrenic irritation from blood under diaphragm can present as RUQ or shoulder pain – think ectopic.
- **"She had a normal TVUS yesterday, so she is fine."** An early ectopic may be invisible on ultrasound until it ruptures. Clinical suspicion overrides imaging.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant) immediately:**

- Any haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg, HR >110, lactate >2 mmol/L)
- GCS <15 or altered mental status
- NEWS2 score ≥5
- Positive β-hCG + collapsed patient
- Suspected ectopic but unable to confirm by TVUS (low hCG) – needs senior US review or diagnostic laparoscopy
- Rh-negative woman with anti-D not yet given
- Patient on anticoagulant therapy (higher risk of massive bleed)

**When to call ICU / crash team:**

- **Cardiorespiratory arrest** – start CPR, call crash team, prepare for emergency laparotomy (peri-mortem caesarean section not applicable here, but if uterus is gravid and >24 weeks, also consider perimortem C-section for maternal survival). Usually ruptured ectopic is early first trimester, so immediate laparotomy to clamp bleeding vessels is the priority.
- **Massive haemorrhage defined as >4 units RBC in 1 hour** – activate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 ratio of RBC, FFP, platelets).
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – reflects severe ischaemia and lactic acidosis requiring ICU monitoring.
- **Need for mechanical ventilation** – for airway protection or ongoing shock.

**Specific thresholds to know:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| SBP | <90 mmHg | IV access, fluid bolus, call senior |
| HR | >110 bpm | Presume hypovolaemia; crossmatch |
| Lactate | >2 mmol/L | Urgent intervention; >4 = ICU |
| Hb | <70 g/L (or drop >30 g/L in 4h) | Transfuse |
| NEWS2 | ≥5 | Escalate to registar; ≥7 = crash team |
| β-hCG not declining 15% by day 1 post-op | Persistent trophoblast | Call senior for methotrexate plan |

**Remember: ruptured ectopic kills by haemorrhage. The sooner you call for help and the more aggressively you control bleeding, the better the outcome. Do not wait for a formal diagnosis – treat the shocked pregnant woman as a ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.**
